exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.title: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.passage: Last winter I tried a local badminton class and found it immediately (1) ...... . I looked online for an (2) ...... for beginner players nearby. (3) ...... , there wasn't one in my town, so I started asking friends for advice. With very little (4) ...... at first, I borrowed some basic (5) ...... and began practising in a school gym. It soon became an (6) ...... way to stay active after work. A few months later, we organised a friendly (7) ...... for new members. Now we even have a part-time (8) ...... and our regular (9) ...... come from several neighbourhood clubs. Last month, one of our players won a silver (10) ...... in a regional tournament.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from INTEREST, so the correct answer is "interesting".
gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Read the whole line in the passage — tense, preposition, or word form must match the surrounding context.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Duration phrases (all day, since, for) often signal present perfect or continuous forms.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: She finds it impossible to put **H** up with people who cheat at games.
items.answer: H – up with people who cheat at games.
acceptedAnswers: H – up with people who cheat at games.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Cannot tolerate → put up with people who cheat.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: Phrasal verbs split across the match — the particle (up, off, out) must collocate with the verb on the left.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: She finds it impossible to put up with people who cheat at games.

wrongOptions.C: Ending C ("out in the second round of the competition.") does not collocate naturally with "She finds it impossible to put".
wrongOptions.F: Ending F ("off team sports lately.") does not collocate naturally with "She finds it impossible to put".
wrongOptions.E: Ending E ("on with the activity while he answered the phone.") does not collocate naturally with "She finds it impossible to put".
wrongOptions.G: Ending G ("the race forward by a week.") does not collocate naturally with "She finds it impossible to put".
wrongOptions.A: Ending A ("up a martial art, to defend yourself.") does not collocate naturally with "She finds it impossible to put".
wrongOptions.B: Ending B ("to swimming as soon as he tried it.") does not collocate naturally with "She finds it impossible to put".
wrongOptions.D: Ending D ("out of the race because of a last minute injury.") does not collocate naturally with "She finds it impossible to put".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not force ending C ("out in the second round of the competition.") — only H completes "She finds it impossible to put" naturally.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Say the full sentence aloud — if it sounds unnatural, try a different ending letter.

items.sentence: The defending champion was knocked **C** out in the second round of the competition.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Eliminated from a tournament → knocked out in the second round.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: Out particles often complete phrasal verbs (went out, split up) — read the full sentence to check meaning.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: The defending champion was knocked out in the second round of the competition.

wrongOptions.H: Ending H ("up with people who cheat at games.") does not collocate naturally with "The defending champion was knocked".
wrongOptions.F: Ending F ("off team sports lately.") does not collocate naturally with "The defending champion was knocked".

wrongOptions.A: Ending A ("up a martial art, to defend yourself.") does not collocate naturally with "The defending champion was knocked".
wrongOptions.B: Ending B ("to swimming as soon as he tried it.") does not collocate naturally with "The defending champion was knocked".
wrongOptions.D: Ending D ("out of the race because of a last minute injury.") does not collocate naturally with "The defending champion was knocked".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not force ending H ("up with people who cheat at games.") — only C completes "The defending champion was knocked" naturally.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Prepositional phrases are fixed chunks — small changes (on/at/for) change the meaning completely.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete each second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete each second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
The tennis match kicked off at noon and the players were still on court at three. **been**
At three o'clock, the players ...... three hours.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is duration up to three o'clock, so the correct answer is "had been playing tennis for".

wrongOptions.been playing tennis for: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had been playing tennis for", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Jake never used to spend so many evenings playing video games. **use**
Jake ...... spend so many evenings playing video games.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past habit, negative, so the correct answer is "did not use to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
Before the game starts, check all the pieces are on the board. **certain**
Before the game starts, ...... all the pieces are on the board.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ensure something, so the correct answer is "make certain that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "make certain that", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Rock climbing can be scary, but after a while you don't mind it. **used**
Rock climbing can be scary, but after a while you ...... it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is become accustomed, so the correct answer is "get used to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "get used to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I ran ten miles in the morning, and then my trainer told me to do a hundred squats! **already**
When my trainer told me to do a hundred squats, I ...... ten miles!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is earlier action, so the correct answer is "had already run".

wrongOptions.already run: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple only shows one past event — use past perfect when one past action happened before another.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect describes an action that happened before another past action.

items.sentence:
As children, we never spent so many afternoons watching TV instead of playing sport. **used**
We ...... so much time indoors when I was young.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past habit, negative, so the correct answer is "never used to spend".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
Practising the guitar takes a great deal of his free time. **up**
His guitar practice ...... a lot of his free time.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is occupy time, so the correct answer is "take up".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "take up", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "take up" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence:
I would rather play chess than draughts. **prefer**
I ...... chess rather than draughts.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is express preference, so the correct answer is "would prefer to play".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "would prefer to play", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I doubt very much that you'll win the tournament. **chance**
I think there's ...... the tournament.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is unlikely to win, so the correct answer is "little chance of your winning".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "little chance of your winning", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.
